record.title: Bette Jane Gulliford | Interview 1 | January 1, 2016
record.transcript:
DUNHAM: And we usually start at the beginning, and so when and where were you born?
GULLIFORD: I was born in Pasadena, California, May 31, 1923.
DUNHAM: And what was your full name?
GULLIFORD: Bette Jane Smallwood. Smallwood is a family name.
DUNHAM: Oh, and how do you spell that?
GULLIFORD: S-M-A-L-L-W-O-O-D [spells].
DUNHAM: Okay, Smallwood, very well. And can you tell us a little bit about growing up in Pasadena--well, actually before that, I'm sorry, can you tell us a little bit about your family history? Do you know when your family first came to the United States and from where?
GULLIFORD: No, I can't. I don't have that in my memory. I just got some papers this last week from my niece that looked up a lot of this.
DUNHAM: Oh, okay, well we can talk about that later then. Do you know your family's ethnic background or what nationality they were originally?
GULLIFORD: Well, Mother's family was German, from what I understand, and Daddy's I'm not sure about. I think--it seems to me there is Dutch or something in that. Mother's parents came from Germany prior to 1882. Mother's maiden name was Ronge. Father was English. His parents came to US prior to 1826.
DUNHAM: What was it like growing up in Pasadena in the twenties and then in the thirties, through the Depression?
GULLIFORD: Oh well, yeah, it was--it was good. It was a--you know, it wasn't such a busy town. We walked everyplace, we did things we wanted to do. You didn't have to worry about it.
DUNHAM: How many siblings did you have?
GULLIFORD: I had--my sister was ten years older than me, and then the two boys in between and then me.
DUNHAM: Okay, so four of you.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, Norma and then Elmer and then Bob and then me. Elmer is Ann's father.
DUNHAM: And where did you live in Pasadena?
GULLIFORD: We lived on 448 North Wilson. [laughing]
DUNHAM: What kind of place did you live in? What was your home like?
GULLIFORD: Oh, it was a nice little three-bedroom place, just like all the other homes around there.
DUNHAM: Okay, and what was life like for your mom while you were growing up? What was an average day like for your mom?
GULLIFORD: Well, she was a homeperson. She kept the house and watched out for us and took care of all of us. Sometimes she'd go down to the print shop and do a little something for my dad if he needed papers put together or something of that sort.
DUNHAM: So your dad ran a print shop. What kind of print shop was that?
GULLIFORD: Well, it was--it was all his own and he did whatever people needed, business cards. He printed programs for theaters even, in those days. And then he'd get free tickets to the movies, so every once in a while one of us kids would get to go free to the movie. [laughing]
DUNHAM: What are your memories of going to the movies in those days?
GULLIFORD: Oh, well that was--it was good. There wasn't so many things to do in those days, you know.
DUNHAM: Was there a big movie house in Pasadena or more than one?
GULLIFORD: We had about--I think it was two movie theaters there in Pasadena. And we went to the closest one that you could walk to, you know.
DUNHAM: Okay, and would it have the newsreels and all of that, back in those early years?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah, I'm sure it did. Yeah.
DUNHAM: Did you work in the print shop at all, growing up?
GULLIFORD: No, I didn't. Daddy would bring things home sometimes that needed to be put together and we'd do it there, but I did not help in the print shop.
DUNHAM: What kind of work would that be, putting the things together?
GULLIFORD: Oh, papers that--a group, you know, that needed to be stapled together. We would assemble them in the right order, and then so he could put them into one thing.
DUNHAM: What was school like?
GULLIFORD: Oh, it was great. I still remember my kindergarten teacher's name, Miss Crandall, and she lived in a little apartment upstairs, downtown in Pasadena there. Once in a while we'd go to see her and visit with her, even years later after we were up in the other grades.
DUNHAM: What was the ethnic makeup of your school at the time? Was it diverse at all? Or it was primarily a variety of white and European?
GULLIFORD: Ninety-nine percent white.
DUNHAM: Do you remember any exceptions, what nationalities they might have been?
GULLIFORD: Not other nationalities particularly. A few black, but not very many in those days.
DUNHAM: Do you know what the black families in town might have done for work, or what types of--
GULLIFORD: I don't know. My daddy probably would know. Of course he got acquainted with a lot of people through the business and knew what they were all about.
DUNHAM: You mentioned your kindergarten teacher. Did you have other favorite teachers or subjects growing up?
GULLIFORD: Well, she was the main one, namewise, that we can remember. Oh--and my classes were all normal and good, you know.
DUNHAM: When you were a little girl did you have an idea of what you wanted to do when you grew up, if you will?
GULLIFORD: No, I don't--not really.
DUNHAM: Did you know any of your grandparents?
GULLIFORD: No, I never met them. When my folks, after I was born, the story I kept hearing was that every time they moved they had a child, so when they came to California and I was born they quit moving. Each of the four children were born in different cities or states.
DUNHAM: Oh, okay--do you know where all your siblings were born?
GULLIFORD: Michigan mainly, I think. I'd still have to look it all up. First was born in Saginaw, Mich; second Dayton, OH, third Bay City Mich; fourth Pasadena, CA.
DUNHAM: And did the print shop business hold up okay throughout the Depression?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah, yeah. Yeah--we didn't, I didn't know we were poor or having a depression, but that's the way that things were, you know. Print Shop definitely had difficulties according to Ann's father.
DUNHAM: Did you grow up seeing the Rose Bowl Parade?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah, always, always saw it. Just that one year that we went down and worked on the floats.
DUNHAM: How old were you when you did that and how did that come about--about?
GULLIFORD: I'm thinking maybe around sixth grade or something like that, because we were a little bit bigger to be able to be told what to do.
DUNHAM: And so what did you do? You worked with the flowers?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: And what was that like? Very intricate?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah, because we had to take flowers all apart and stick the pieces on different parts of the floats where they were trying to make complexions or you know, eyes and things on some of the characters on the floats.
DUNHAM: Right, right. Was it fun?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: Where would you watch the parade?
GULLIFORD: Right there on Colorado and--Mentor was the street where my dad's shop was, and we would just go down a few doors down and stand there and watch the parade.
DUNHAM: Okay, in those days would you have to get there extra early to get a place?
GULLIFORD: Well, we'd get down there pretty early yeah, so we'd keep a spot where we could sit down, put a chair or a box or something to, a tricycle or something to sit on so you could watch the parade.
DUNHAM: Yeah, before the US joined the war, were you following news of the war in Europe and abroad?
GULLIFORD: No.
DUNHAM: Did you, aside from the movies and those types of things, what did you do for fun? Did you have a radio, play games--or what kinds of things did you do with your friends?
GULLIFORD: Well, my girlfriend and I would walk down to one of the main libraries quite often, and we'd get our books and read quite a bit. Where our house was we had a--there was a vacant lot down on the corner and then behind our houses. And we'd get out there and play games or go dig around in the dirt looking for things, and play baseball and stuff. We liked to--one girlfriend I had and I, we made it a game of trying to climb every tree on our street, going all the way down the street and then all the way back up. We managed to climb them all and get up in them.
DUNHAM: Wow, wow, so you were an adventurous girl.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, pretty much.
DUNHAM: What kinds of trees, do you recall?
GULLIFORD: Oh, I don't remember what they were, but there weren't great big tall ones, you know, they were regular ones that branched out.
DUNHAM: And so you graduated high school in June of '41? Was it 1941, before Pearl Harbor?
GULLIFORD: Yes, yeah.
DUNHAM: And so what were your plans after high school graduation?
GULLIFORD: Well, we knew we were going to go to work through the summer months at least, and just go look for work. [laughing]
DUNHAM: Yeah, did you--so did you--do you remember what you did that summer?
GULLIFORD: Well, yeah, my girlfriend, her sister, and I got a job down in--I think it was Alhambra at the airport down there, and that's where the little two-wing airplanes were, you know, the family planes and that stuff. They taught us how to take the wings and stretch the material over the wings and how--I forget how we attached to--sewed them somehow.
DUNHAM: So to cover the wings when they were in storage kind of thing?
GULLIFORD: And then we would goop them so it would make them hard.
DUNHAM: These are planes that are being made? Or these are existing planes that you're--
GULLIFORD: Getting repairs, yeah, mainly, in a regular airport, airfield.
DUNHAM: And this is in the summer of '41 before the US joined the war?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, before we went there. And that was just pretty much a temporary thing too.
DUNHAM: Okay, so how long did you do that? Do you recall? Did you do that up until--
GULLIFORD: Several, a number of months, and then when the job opened up there to--I've got it all written down someplace here.
DUNHAM: Yeah, well I know it's in the article too. It says that on December 5, two days before the Pearl Harbor attack, you guys went to work at Monitor Products?
GULLIFORD: Monitor, yeah.
DUNHAM: So how did you find out about the jobs at Monitor Products?
GULLIFORD: Well, they must have advertised, I think, is the only reason we--because there were a couple of other girls that went in.
DUNHAM: And so what was the job at Monitor?
GULLIFORD: Well, it was very intricate. We would sit at a table and we'd--they'd bring us these quartz crystals that were cut down to as small as they could get them on the big machines, and then we would take them and we'd have a dish there with carborundum and a dish with water and we'd put it on a little glass plate, mix it up, and then we would take that little crystal and grind it down and then we would wipe it off and stick it in a little plug-in and plug it in a machine, and it would tell the numbers. We'd have to get it down to a certain degree to get them thin enough, small enough, to fit into the walkie-talkie radios that they carried out in the fields in the war, you know.
DUNHAM: So that's what the crystals were for, they were for the--
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So the numbers were regarding the size? Or something else?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, the size.
DUNHAM: What type of equipment did you use to grind them down?
GULLIFORD: Just our fingers and--
DUNHAM: Oh really?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, on the wet kind of carborundum.
DUNHAM: So they were soft in that--
GULLIFORD: Yeah, that was soft. The crystals were hard, of course, like glass, you know.
DUNHAM: So was it hard work?
GULLIFORD: Well, it was--we had to be right. We couldn't just slop around at it. We had to know what we were doing. And then eventually I got to where I was the one testing the ones that were all put together in a machine, to be sure that they registered what they were supposed to. And if they didn't I'd have to send them back and they'd have to take them out of the little holder and redo them a little bit and get it down to where it was supposed to go.
DUNHAM: Do you remember how many people's crystals you were testing, about?
GULLIFORD: I don't remember how many. I know I would just--quite a few, because we were--I think there were the five of us girls in--
DUNHAM: Who you'd known coming in, had all--
GULLIFORD: Three of them I knew, the other two were just hired in at the same time.
DUNHAM: Okay, well backing up just a minute, do you remember applying for the job and what that process was like?
GULLIFORD: No, I really don't. I just remember sitting there working at them.
DUNHAM: Okay, do you remember that first day on the job, if it was surprising or challenging in any particular way?
GULLIFORD: No, not particularly one special day.
DUNHAM: It was just a small group of you, all women working together? There weren't men doing it as well?
GULLIFORD: I don't think they were, on the crystals. They were--the men were back in the end of the room where the saws were, and they were cutting the quartz rocks down and splitting them and getting them down into size.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Do you remember what you were paid?
GULLIFORD: No, I don't.
DUNHAM: Do you think it was much more than you were getting in your previous job?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I think--I don't remember what we got on that first one. It was kind of a, for us, a kind of a play thing--
DUNHAM: Yeah, a summer young-person's job.
GULLIFORD: --that we didn't pay much attention to. We just, in those days, you know--
DUNHAM: So you started this job just before Pearl Harbor [was] attacked?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: What do you remember hearing about the December 7 attack on Pearl Harbor?
GULLIFORD: Well, everybody was up running around, talking to each other. I think we even went outside and looked, you know. We were still there--when was it, '45?
DUNHAM: At the end of the war?
GULLIFORD: The end.
DUNHAM: Oh, so you worked this job--
GULLIFORD: And then when we heard that the war was over, the whole--everybody in the shop went outside and raised their arms and yelled and whooped it up. [laughing]
DUNHAM: Yeah, so you worked at this job all through the war?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, through that, yeah.
DUNHAM: From just before the US engaged till at the end. Wow. Well, do you remember--on Pearl Harbor, when you first heard of the US being attacked, do you remember that? That was at a Sunday. Would you have been working then?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: You worked--okay. What days did you work, or shifts, or days?
GULLIFORD: Well, we were working--we worked the day shift on that job and yeah, the regular eight-to-four probably I think it was.
DUNHAM: Were you in a union?
GULLIFORD: No, no union, no.
DUNHAM: No, okay. And so did you--how long before, do you remember how long before you became the inspector, about? Since you were doing that same type of work all four years?
GULLIFORD: Well, making them pretty much the whole time, and then I guess the last year or two I was doing the finishing up on them.
DUNHAM: Do you remember who was in the inspector position before? Or what--or your boss. What was your boss like at that time?
GULLIFORD: We had men bosses, but I don't remember disliking anybody. [laughing]
DUNHAM: Did you get evaluations and/or raises during the war? How did that type of thing work?
GULLIFORD: We must have gotten a nickel or a dime raise once in a while. I can't remember.
DUNHAM: And I'm sorry, I didn't ask--where were you working exactly? What city was it in? Was it in Pasadena?
GULLIFORD: South Pasadena, yeah.
DUNHAM: South Pasadena, okay. And then were you still living at home with your family?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay, all through the war years. And so how did you get to work?
GULLIFORD: Drove, had a car. Yeah, we had a car.
DUNHAM: Oh, okay, you had your own car or a family car?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I had a car.
DUNHAM: When did you buy your car and what car did you buy?
GULLIFORD: The first one was--what was it--it had a rumble seat.
DUNHAM: Okay, what's a rumble seat?
GULLIFORD: Where--it's instead of a trunk it's--the seat, the lid goes up and there's a seat on the back of the car. It must have been a Ford or Chevy, because in those days--
DUNHAM: Did you have it while you were still in high school or after you started working?
GULLIFORD: No, after I went to work.
DUNHAM: Okay. So how did things change in Pasadena during the war? How did they change?
GULLIFORD: Well, we were so busy working and just having fun on the side, that I don't remember things really changing that much.
DUNHAM: Well, tell me about the fun on the side--what types of things, as a young adult now, a young woman, were you doing for fun?
GULLIFORD: Well, we started--I'm sure it was while we were--yeah, '41 I was eighteen. During those years we started going over--I drove, and my two girlfriends, it was the one was the same grade and then her sister, but we'd go over to Hollywood on Saturday night, to the Palladium. That was the big deal for us, go dancing to the big bands.
DUNHAM: Right, did you see--who did you see? Glenn Miller, Tommy Dorsey?
GULLIFORD: No, Glenn Miller was flying, he was in the service.
DUNHAM: Oh right, thank you.
GULLIFORD: He was about the only one that didn't come. But the Dorsey brothers, Benny Goodman--what was his name Carle, possibly Frankie Carle--he was a piano player. And during that time we heard about a place down in Santa Monica, by the ocean there. Lick's Pier was the place, out on the pier. There was a guy hardly anybody heard of in those days. Oh, I'm going to say--
DUNHAM: Well, if it comes to you, just tell us later.
GULLIFORD: Very popular.
DUNHAM: Okay, a band leader or a singer?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, band--he was--Lester Kaiser, no. Oh, he was so popular!
DUNHAM: Lawrence Welk?
GULLIFORD: Lawrence Welk! Yeah, and that's--I don't know, why did it go--
DUNHAM: Oh, okay, yeah. Sure, he had the TV show many years later.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, that's before people even heard of him or knew who he was and what was going on.
DUNHAM: What do you remember about seeing Lawrence Welk back in Santa Monica?
GULLIFORD: Well, good music! We always liked it, and when we went to Hollywood, there at the Palladium, the Stage Door Canteen was just around the corner from the Palladium ballroom, and the movie stars would be in there enticing the servicemen to come up and talk to them and get their autograph. And then when they'd do that then they'd come on into the Palladium there to dance. And so we never sat out the dances--all those servicemen available and regular boys too.
DUNHAM: What was dating like at that time?
GULLIFORD: Well, unfortunately, when I was seventeen I got involved and got married.
DUNHAM: Oh, okay.
GULLIFORD: But we really didn't make that much of it and got a divorce before too long.
DUNHAM: Oh, so--okay. Was that before you graduated high school?
GULLIFORD: I was still in school, that last year of school. We rented a little apartment, but it just--I don't know.
DUNHAM: It wasn't meant to be.
GULLIFORD: It wasn't meant to be. Well, we were just kids, so I got out of that one.
DUNHAM: Okay, did you have any children with your first husband?
GULLIFORD: No, no.
DUNHAM: So you were married and divorced before this work started? Or was it while you were--before the war work?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I think I was through with him before we went to work there, yeah.
DUNHAM: Anything else you want to say about that early, short marriage?
GULLIFORD: No. It just--my folks didn't approve, but they didn't kick me out or anything. They accepted and tried to help me get through it.
DUNHAM: So then after you'd had that experience, what was dating like after that, while you had a little money and a car and able to go to the dances? Did you do some dating?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah--I'd find different ones to go out with for a while. But it--and working too, at the same time, why, you know, you've only got so much time.
DUNHAM: Sure, sure, but you were young and it sounded like you were out on the town a bit. Where would you go to eat?
GULLIFORD: Oh, gosh--I don't even remember where we did dine around there. We didn't--I don't think we even went out to eat all that much, and if you did it probably would just be a hamburger.
DUNHAM: You mostly would just eat at home then.
GULLIFORD: Money--money was scarce, you know, for the boyfriends even. [laughing]
DUNHAM: What about rationing? Was there any problem having enough gas for your car?
GULLIFORD: Well, we had to have gas stamps, so any place we could borrow them, or they would give them to us, why--the folks, my folks, or my girlfriend's folks. Any way we could get the gas stamps in order to get over to Hollywood and back.
DUNHAM: Yeah, about how much did that take, to get to Hollywood and back?
GULLIFORD: Oh, I don't remember how many miles it was over there--forty miles maybe, over into Hollywood there.
DUNHAM: And was that mostly on the weekends? Or it could be any night you might go over there?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, but mainly Saturday night.
DUNHAM: Saturday night, okay, okay.
GULLIFORD: And the big bands would be there.
DUNHAM: Yeah, and so during the week would you listen to the radio, or what types of things would you do in the evening?
GULLIFORD: Oh, my girlfriends would come over or I'd go over to one of them and we played games. I forget just what--
DUNHAM: Cards, or?
GULLIFORD: Cards and--we didn't play checkers. We played--well, Monopoly got to be a big game in those days. That was one of them. And just sit around and listen to the radio too, you know. No TV, of course.
DUNHAM: Right! What kinds of things would you listen to on the radio?
GULLIFORD: Oh, Saturday night we'd always listen to the dance, the music. They'd play the popular songs of the week.
DUNHAM: Would you listen to the president's fireside chats when they were on?
GULLIFORD: I don't think we did. I'm sure my folks would.
DUNHAM: Were your girlfriend and her sister living with their folks? Or did they have their own place?
GULLIFORD: Oh, they were still living at home with their folks, yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay, in Pasadena as well?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay, well, did you have a sense of patriotism while you were working this job, working on the crystals? What was the feeling of the time?
GULLIFORD: Well, then yeah, for that yeah. We were very devoted to what we were doing, and it was important and we didn't try to fool around and mess it up.
DUNHAM: So I know you were in a small group. Was the company itself a pretty big company? Did they have events? Or was it a relatively small company?
GULLIFORD: It was a small company.
DUNHAM: Okay, okay.
GULLIFORD: It was the only one that was there in South Pasadena.
DUNHAM: And did you interact with the male workers at lunch time or on breaks? What was that like?
GULLIFORD: No, we'd--we'd just, it was in a small town there, and I'm sure we just went out for lunch and back again.
DUNHAM: Did you get to see the crystals in the walkie-talkies, how it actually worked? Did you have a sense of that? She told me she had a photo a soldier friend sent her showing the radio with these crysals. If we ever find it, I'll send it to you.
GULLIFORD: No, the last, they would be put once--for testing them we would have the little plug-in container that they would put into the radios, but they would leave the top open on it, so we can put the crystal in it and plug it in. And if it didn't read right then we would pull it out and take the crystal out and do it some more and then put it in and test it back and forth. But when they went out of there, then they were sealed into the little plug-in mechanism that fit into the walkie-talkies.
DUNHAM: Were there ever any--or what kinds of injuries might have happened at the place you worked? Was there ever any problem with your fingers or cutting--that type of thing?
GULLIFORD: No, we didn't seem to have any. I know I didn't.
DUNHAM: So it wasn't dangerous, the job you were doing?
GULLIFORD: No, no.
DUNHAM: Was it monotonous? Were you doing a very high number of them each day?
GULLIFORD: No, I don't think we ever got bored with it.
DUNHAM: Okay, it kept your attention.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, keeping you--being active the whole time, it kept you--
DUNHAM: Did your salary go up during the course of the war?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah. It went up some but not a great deal. The normal raises that people would get then.
DUNHAM: Did you have friends that you knew were doing other types of jobs, like working in the airplane factories or elsewhere, female friends?
GULLIFORD: No, mainly the ones I had were with me, working with me. I forget what the others were doing, but--
DUNHAM: Did you see the Rosie the Riveter images while you were doing this work or at the movie theaters? Were you aware of that symbolism at the time?
GULLIFORD: Not really. Like I say, so many things are so hard to remember.
DUNHAM: Yeah, then from now.
GULLIFORD: I don't think it was an emblem in those days.
DUNHAM: What do you remember, once the war started about--like would you go to the movies then and see the newsreels? What was that like?
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah. Yeah, we'd go up to the movies, usually a Saturday afternoon.
DUNHAM: Okay, well what else--we mentioned a little bit about rationing with gas. Did rationing--were there other things that were hard to come by during the war because of rationing, in terms of meat--heard stories about nylons, other types of things?
GULLIFORD: No, not really. We seemed to be pretty comfortable and hardly even knowing it was--people were hungry and--
DUNHAM: What did you wear to work?
GULLIFORD: Well, that was just regular clothing in those days. We didn't all wear pants like we do now.
DUNHAM: So you were still wearing dresses to work.
GULLIFORD: Dresses, yeah, and stockings.
DUNHAM: Did you see--again, back to--I know you didn't have friends who worked at the airplane plants and all, but some women were wearing uniforms, pants and other things, to different jobs. Did you see--
GULLIFORD: Well, you'd see them around town wearing different things.
DUNHAM: So did you sense any fashion changes, per se, during the war?
GULLIFORD: Not enough that made any difference to us.
DUNHAM: Okay. And did you hear, see and/or hear of the zoot-suiters during the war years?
GULLIFORD: Oh, that sounds familiar.
DUNHAM: But did you have any perspective on that, of Latinos and Latinas and their fashion and/or anything else you heard about it in Southern California at the time?
GULLIFORD: I don't think we even associated with them for any reason.
DUNHAM: Okay. Did you participate in a victory garden?
GULLIFORD: Well, we had a--yeah, my daddy had a garden out in the back there, yeah. But we also had a big fig tree and a walnut tree and avocado. So we had all those fresh things.
DUNHAM: Nice!
GULLIFORD: Mother made great pickled figs and things like that.
DUNHAM: Oh! Pickled figs.
GULLIFORD: We'd sit and crack walnuts so we'd have--for cookies and stuff.
DUNHAM: How was your father's print shop business affected by the war? Did he have more opportunities?
GULLIFORD: He had plenty of business, from all that I know. Of course like I say, he printed for other businesses around town, the car dealers and the movie theaters, restaurants--any place that needed programs or things to get by with. She was the youngest in the family and her parents were not sharing with her their difficulities. Like other families, her dad would barter printing services for things they needed.
DUNHAM: What were your older siblings doing during the war?
GULLIFORD: They were in school. The three older siblings had graduated by 1937. The two oldest were married. Her younger brother Bob went into the service in 1941. He was in the invasion of Utah Beach and served under General Patton. His unit stormed Hitler's home and Bob kept a key that had been in the bedroom door lock. He is pictured on the back cover of The Last Good War with the key between his teeth. His story is in the book.
DUNHAM: Oh, in university? Her sister when to beauty school. The boys didn't have higher education.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, well--my sister had a beauty shop of her own.
DUNHAM: Where was that?
GULLIFORD: It was in Pasadena too, on Hill Street. She would keep my hair cut and take care of my mother and I, and the boys would go to the barber's.
DUNHAM: Cece. [a cat] [laughing]
GULLIFORD: She's getting brave now. [laughter]
DUNHAM: Did you date any servicemen?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, which one was that--yeah, I did date one, and I'm trying to think, put that together.
DUNHAM: Okay, well if that comes to you, let me know.
GULLIFORD: Where was he stationed? I've put it out of my mind, but yeah, I know I did date one serviceman for a while.
DUNHAM: Okay. And I didn't ask you, but did you go to church growing up? And if so, what religion?
GULLIFORD: Just when I was little, and then I got away from it and didn't go back again for a good many years.
DUNHAM: What church did you--
GULLIFORD: Methodist.
DUNHAM: And is that what you returned to then, later?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, when I did go back it was Methodist. That's when I married my kids' dad. He was--his family was pretty churchy.
DUNHAM: Okay, and about when did you get married the second time? Was that well after the war?
GULLIFORD: Well, I was married more than once. [laughing]
DUNHAM: Well, when was your second marriage? Was that during the war?
GULLIFORD: [pause] I'm trying to think now. Golly, I'm going to have to dig out papers.
DUNHAM: Was it with your second husband that you first had children?
GULLIFORD: No.
DUNHAM: Okay.
GULLIFORD: I found, when I finally got pregnant, I found out that I had to have them Caesarean, because I couldn't pass more than a pound or two baby. So that's--after I had the two kids, why they tied the tubes and that was it. I couldn't have any more.
DUNHAM: And that was your third marriage?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Yeah.
GULLIFORD: In between there I was married. He finally was cheating and doing things that shouldn't be done, so I finally got rid of him.
DUNHAM: Oh, I'm sorry.
GULLIFORD: But that's the way things go.
DUNHAM: Yeah, yeah. But it was--did you know many other folks who had divorced? It must have been hard, maybe, in that time.
GULLIFORD: It wasn't a good thing to do. But by keeping working--
DUNHAM: Yeah, keeping independent.
GULLIFORD: Then the next job I had when the kids were born--well, Danny was--Johnny was two and Danny was two months when I split up with the guy, and I had to go back to work. So I went to work there in La Puente at the, making--sewing. And we made--that's where we made panels, waterproof panels that fit over wooden framed Quonset huts. And they were all shipped out to the war zones for-- Bette was an excellent seamstress. She made most of her clothes and also outfits for my mom.
DUNHAM: This is during the Korean War?
GULLIFORD: During the--yeah, so that the servicemen could sleep in them and live in them. The panels were about so wide, {an armish?}. They had, on my sewing machine it was about this big. They were--it was heavy sewing and they were filled with fiberglass foam and stuff.
DUNHAM: Wow, so how did you get that job? How did you find it? Do you recall?
GULLIFORD: Oh, I'm sure they advertised for it, and by that time I was living down in La Puente. I'd gone over and lived with my sister in her travel trailer. She'd take care of the boys in the daytime when I went to work, till I got married and stuff.
DUNHAM: Okay, okay. So after the husband who fathered your children you--
GULLIFORD: Yeah, so--
DUNHAM: So did he have any contact with the kids after that?
GULLIFORD: No, no.
DUNHAM: Okay, so you were a single mother. And was this the sister who had the salon?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So was she able to keep them at the salon? Or she--
GULLIFORD: No, she was--that was a little bit later then. She'd married and they settled down out in La Puente.
DUNHAM: And she had children of her own as well?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, but they were in school.
DUNHAM: Okay, okay. They were a little older?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So what was--it must have been hard, even with that help, to be working full-time and then coming home to a two-month-old and two-year-old.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, well, I don't know how I did it, but with the salary I made I had a live-in housekeeper, so that I had to be to work at six in the morning, and that's pretty early to have to get up and go to work.
DUNHAM: Sure, sure.
GULLIFORD: So I couldn't handle the kids at that hour in the morning, because they'd be sleeping. So I got the live-in housekeeper, so that she would be there and take care of the kids in the daytime and get them up and keep them in the yard there.
DUNHAM: And then how late did your shift go till?
GULLIFORD: I think it was two o'clock.
DUNHAM: Okay. And then--and you were driving.
GULLIFORD: Yeah. I had an old Chevy in those days--an old one! [laughing] Sometimes I couldn't get the dumb thing started, so there happened to be a guy in the neighborhood that drove a taxi, so I'd call him and he'd bring his car, his taxi over, and give me a push and get me started so I could get to work.
DUNHAM: Yeah, it sounds like it must have been challenging times to put it all together.
GULLIFORD: Oh, it was, it was.
DUNHAM: And be a young mother. Do you think having the work experience you got during the war helped you be independent, strong enough to negotiate all that?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I think so. Yeah, I'm sure, yeah.
DUNHAM: Do you think you might have stayed in either of the marriages without those opportunities?
GULLIFORD: Oh--a possibility. Of course when you both work and you're busy, why it's sometimes hard to relate like you should. [laughing]
DUNHAM: So then you married again--and how was that? Or how much later did you marry again?
GULLIFORD: Oh boy--
DUNHAM: Or how old were the boys?
GULLIFORD: I'm trying to think back now to their dad. Well, they were little when--yeah, it was Wally that, when they were two and two months, and he was overboard with religion. He'd be out in the streets at night trying to convert people or he'd be in the bar trying to convert drinkers and that kind of stuff. And it just got to where he wasn't--he wasn't there when he was supposed to be. He was gone so much and wouldn't change. So anyway, but--
DUNHAM: So he just got kind of fanatical about religion?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, so I just--
DUNHAM: Had he been a drinker himself previously?
GULLIFORD: Not too much. Some, but not much.
DUNHAM: Did you yourself drink some?
GULLIFORD: In those days some beers, something, you know.
DUNHAM: So his religion was a source of conflict?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, a good part of it, yeah.
DUNHAM: Well, so then did you raise the boys on your own all through the years?
GULLIFORD: Quite a few years, yeah. I'm trying to think when I did get remarried again. We were together a long time.
DUNHAM: How did you meet that husband, who you stayed together with a long time?
GULLIFORD: It must have been at one of the dances. I'm trying to think.
DUNHAM: So you were still able to go to those dance clubs on occasion?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, and on some weekends. But--
DUNHAM: Did you stay in touch after the war with your girlfriend and her sister?
GULLIFORD: Off and on, but never really that close anymore with the girls. I'd keep too busy working in family and stuff to--
DUNHAM: Yeah, you had a lot of responsibilities.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: During the war years did you know--I know it wasn't talked about as openly as today, but did you know of any gays or lesbians at the time?
GULLIFORD: No.
DUNHAM: I had another--because with your German ancestry, was that ever known or was that ever an issue because of the Germans in the war?
GULLIFORD: No.
DUNHAM: Yeah, no. Did you know any German Americans, Italian Americans, or Japanese Americans who were incarcerated or that you knew had been prisoners of war? Being on the West Coast especially, I guess, did you know of any Japanese Americans?
GULLIFORD: No, I'm thinking--it seems to me there was something that happened there, because there were a couple of Japanese families in the area, in Pasadena there. I guess nothing that was serious enough that I remember.
DUNHAM: Yeah, well if you weren't close to them you might not have noticed, but they probably were incarcerated during the war then, after Pearl Harbor.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I would think so, yeah.
DUNHAM: You don't recall what kind of work or businesses they had?
GULLIFORD: No.
DUNHAM: Okay, well anything else about the war years that we didn't cover that you might like to share with us, about things that were going on or happening?
GULLIFORD: Well, the next job I had, when I was living in--well, let's see, where were we?
DUNHAM: Well, yeah, you did talk about--so after World War II, during the Korean War--
GULLIFORD: I moved up to--
DUNHAM: Ridge--
GULLIFORD: Up to Ridgecrest.
DUNHAM: Ridgecrest, in the sixties.
GULLIFORD: My husband, at that time, worked for United Parcel. They needed a driver to come up in the--locate in the desert area so they could go on up to Lone Pine and Bishop, the northern towns on the main highway, 395 I think it was then. So that moved us up to Ridgecrest, and for a while we had, on the side--a little beer bar. And then we went into--got a gas station, a Chevron gas station. And just--I was doing the books and he was doing the pumping.
DUNHAM: Okay, and what was running the beer bar like, if you don't mind me asking about that?
GULLIFORD: Well, it was pretty good fun. We got acquainted with--it was local people that would come in there daily.
DUNHAM: This is in Ridgecrest?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, and would sit at certain spots. Sometimes if I saw them get out of the car, why I'd--when they got into their seat I'd have their beer waiting for them there. [laughing]
DUNHAM: Ever any challenges there of folks who got in fights or any of that kind of thing?
GULLIFORD: No, we didn't have that kind.
DUNHAM: It was a pretty calm group.
GULLIFORD: We had some pool tables in there, and the guys would keep busy. And we had pinball machines in there too, and it was a beer--because it was a pool hall, kids could come in. So sometimes I'd be tending bar there and I'd hear that pinball machine making a strange noise, trying to react to something going on, and I'd have to go over and see, and they're trying to jiggle it and get things to work that weren't supposed to. So we finally just drew the line and quit letting them come in. [laughing]
DUNHAM: All right, well--but if that's as rowdy as it got in the bar, that's not too bad I guess.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, that's about all.
DUNHAM: Did you already know how to tend bar, or did you learn on the job?
GULLIFORD: No, we learned on the--yeah.
DUNHAM: Was that fun?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, it wasn't bad at all, really. It was busy, and of course being a small town up there you had the regular customers.
DUNHAM: But then you switched to a gas station, or did you do both?
GULLIFORD: We did the--we had the gas station first and then got the bar, yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay.
GULLIFORD: Well, then while we were there, then it didn't--sometimes I wasn't busy enough, so I went to work at the Naval Weapons Center there in Ridgecrest. [a cat meows]
DUNHAM: Yeah, that was in about '63? Is that--
GULLIFORD: Ouch!
DUNHAM: Oops, you okay?
GULLIFORD: Ouch. My legs are all black and blue where she digs in when she--
DUNHAM: The cat, Cece, jumped up there, jumped off quickly. Well, you went to work at the--what were you doing at the Naval Weapons Center?
GULLIFORD: We read film from the--that they would have, the cameras out on the testing fields and testing areas, wherever they would test. A lot of it was right there in Ridgecrest, because they had thousands of acres up there, where they would drop their bombs or they would fly the planes low and all kinds of things going on. They'd have--sometimes six, seven, eight, ten--maybe a dozen cameras out on the fields, filming all this action going on, because a lot of it was testing new bombs and new ways of releasing them and all of that. So they'd had all these cameras, and then we would read that film. I've got, somewhere I've got a picture of it.
DUNHAM: Oh, I'd love to see that after we're done. What do you mean read the film? What would you--
GULLIFORD: Well, we had crosshairs and we would put it on, like a bomb on an aircraft. We'd put the crosshairs on it underneath the aircraft, and when it released then we would follow it down to impact. We had two dials and we'd punch in the both dials, the elevation and--
DUNHAM: So you're recording the data off of the photographs of the bomb tests?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: And do you know what kind of bombs these were?
GULLIFORD: Oh, I used to know, remember quite a few of them. It wasn't too many years ago I recognized one that they had mentioned in the news that we had worked on--
DUNHAM: That you had used--
GULLIFORD: --when they were new.
DUNHAM: So did you get special training to do this work? Or how did it--
GULLIFORD: Well, they trained us as we hired in. And then I got to be lead lady of that group, to where I would have to be sure to keep the girls busy, have film lined up for them to put in their machines to run through and read. We would--
DUNHAM: These are still photos? Or moving image--
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: No, still photos.
GULLIFORD: Still photos, yeah.
DUNHAM: And it was all women who were doing the reading of the photos?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, in the reading part. Men were in different parts of the building doing different jobs around there.
DUNHAM: And you were civilian?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay.
GULLIFORD: But we did have a--
DUNHAM: Clearance.
GULLIFORD: --secret clearance.
DUNHAM: Yeah, so what--
GULLIFORD: Because of what we were working on.
DUNHAM: Right, so were there specific--there were specific rules about secrecy around that, I assume.
GULLIFORD: Oh yeah.
DUNHAM: Were you--how close were you to where the bombs were being tested? Were you nervous at all about--
GULLIFORD: On the same property, but of course they were farther out and around.
DUNHAM: Did they tell you anything about it, if there was any risk?
GULLIFORD: No. And some things were out at sea too, or different places that they would bring in.
DUNHAM: Oh, bring the footage in from--
GULLIFORD: Sometimes they'd bring it in for some other--an army group or some place, you know, that we would do film for. And it would be--it would print out on cards and then that would be printed into books.
DUNHAM: The data?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Okay, the data you were entering.
GULLIFORD: So that--then the people that were engineering all of this, then they could read what happened.
DUNHAM: Okay, you didn't have to do any analysis of the data. You just entered it, and it went on.
GULLIFORD: We just did the dirty-work part. [laughter]
DUNHAM: So was that work--was it engaging or did it get monotonous doing that?
GULLIFORD: No, it was interesting and it was serious work, so we all paid attention. We didn't goof around with the job, not like you could do on other types of work.
DUNHAM: Were you still working the bar at night?
GULLIFORD: No, no, then I went full-time.
DUNHAM: And was it a relatively good income?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, a pretty decent income and got a raise a few times.
DUNHAM: And your husband of the time, was he working as well?
GULLIFORD: Well, he was running the service--well, what was he doing? Running the service station too, at the time, and that part of it. Well, what happened--then eventually I split up with him.
DUNHAM: Before the boys were grown?
GULLIFORD: And the boys were still--no, they were going to school then, so they were in school mainly while I was working.
DUNHAM: Okay. Did you marry again after that?
GULLIFORD: Finally--[laughing]--
DUNHAM: Oh, that's okay. I'm just--I'm just curious, because I see you're an independent woman.
GULLIFORD: --Really going--
DUNHAM: I'm curious also because you have these at least three different wartime-type jobs. Was that a coincidence? Or was there any particular--
GULLIFORD: They just happened to turn up in that order, and that's the jobs I took. But actually, I worked through all the wars. So that's kind of an accomplishment, you know.
DUNHAM: So this job went through '69, is that right? Or did it end? How did this job end at the Naval Weapons Center?
GULLIFORD: Well, I quit. I retired off of that. And what am I doing now, down here? [laughing]
DUNHAM: So do you remember--yeah, so in '69 you retired, you were able to retire from there?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, I think--what did I do then, stay home or not?
DUNHAM: Were you still in--what is it, the Ridgecrest area or China Lake?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, at that time, yeah. We moved up here in '85--'87, '85?
DUNHAM: You with the boys? Or they're grown then?
GULLIFORD: They're grown and married, and with my husband again, the same--which one--
DUNHAM: The same one you ran the bar with and all?
GULLIFORD: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Oh, so you got back--
GULLIFORD: So anyway, that's--
DUNHAM: Okay, that's fine. On again/off again a little bit.
GULLIFORD: Just too much.
DUNHAM: Okay, okay.
GULLIFORD: That's some of what I forget on purpose.
DUNHAM: All right, well I understand. I don't mean to harp on it. Sometimes it's just interesting to look at gender relations and even marriage and divorce, because prior to World War II it was probably much less common, although you had an early marriage and divorce. But to have divorce--so just curious in some ways about your experiences, and if women don't have work, like you did, then it's very hard to have the option really, to divorce, without relying on family, and so--and it sounds like you were better off, for at least those early ones, to be able to move on.
GULLIFORD: I was always busy working. My husband--we moved up here, he passed away in 20ll. I had my stroke in '10 and then '11 then he passed way. And '12 then I came in here.
DUNHAM: Okay, yeah. So you've been here--
GULLIFORD: But that--well, that's part of it. It was such a massive stroke that it's made me forget a lot of things too.
DUNHAM: Yeah, well I'm sorry we didn't meet you before then, but I think you've done very well today, and so I really appreciate your sharing all that you have. Is there anything else you'd like to share with us about those early years, the World War II years, or anything else before we close?
GULLIFORD: Well, I don't know. I just always liked my jobs and tried to do the best I could. Like I say that last one, and I was lead lady over about--we'd have eight, ten, twelve machines going, so I had to keep things moving all the time. Of course I had supervisors too, you know, to--
DUNHAM: Right, but it sounds like at two of the jobs you had you ultimately became the lead.
GULLIFORD: Yeah, the lead, yeah.
DUNHAM: What does it mean to you now, to be recognized at this late date for your role as a Rosie the Riveter, if you will?
GULLIFORD: Well, I like it. I'm proud of it. [laughing]
DUNHAM: What are some things that you hope this generation or future generations take from your experiences during World War II and beyond?
GULLIFORD: Well, keeping a good job I think is important. Try to behave yourself while you're doing it. [laughing]
DUNHAM: What do you think of in terms of the work you did and paving the way, in some way, for women in the workforce?
GULLIFORD: Well, I think so many things they're capable of doing, that if they would try it. They don't always have to sit behind a register or be in a bank or something like that. There are other things available that are interesting. Well, the last, we were--in 1987--in '95 we moved to Sutherlin, we're in Pine View Senior Mobile Park, and every Monday morning we would get together in the clubhouse, the girls, and sew. And we made quilts for places like this, for the senior homes, and for the preschoolers. We managed to keep the fire department, our fire department and police station supplied, so when they'd go out on a wreck they'd have a throw quilt, about that size, to throw around patients that are in the wrecks. And then we would take scrappy pieces and put them together and we kept the shelter, the animal shelter supplied, because they'd put them in with the cats and dogs so they'd have something special to cuddle up in and dig at, you know--chew on! [laughing]
DUNHAM: Very nice. I forgot to ask you on camera, but going back, you changed your name, the spelling of your name--can you tell us when you did that and the story of that?
GULLIFORD: I don't remember just which year, but I was still in grade school, I'm sure, yeah.
DUNHAM: Oh, okay, okay. Was it your idea to change it?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: And so tell us why you changed--
GULLIFORD: Well, I just thought it looked--her name was Bette and it looked better than having the y on there.
DUNHAM: How was yours originally spelled?
GULLIFORD: It was spelled with a y.
DUNHAM: B-E-T-T-Y [spells]?
GULLIFORD: T-T-Y. But she was the only one at the time that, Bette Davis, the movie star, and so I just started changing it and that's the way it was.
DUNHAM: And your parents were fine with you changing it?
GULLIFORD: Yeah, yeah. No, they never fussed about it. [laughing]. I found out after my parents passed that my dad's middle name on his birth cert was Lewis, but at some time he changed it to Louis. Funny how they changed their names as they pleased back then.
DUNHAM: Excellent. Well, thank you so much, Bette. We really appreciate you taking the time to share your stories with us today. It has been an honor and a privilege to meet you here.
GULLIFORD: Well, thank you. I'm still just a normal little, old lady! [laughter]
DUNHAM: Yeah, absolutely--but we really appreciate it. You've had a very interesting life.
[End of Interview]
